name: Moroi
description: The Moroi are undead creatures in Romanian folklore, distinct from vampires but sharing some characteristics. They are believed to be the restless spirits of the dead, particularly those who died unjustly, violently, or without proper rites. The Moroi are often depicted as possessing supernatural powers, including the ability to drain life force, control the weather, and shapeshift. Unlike the classic image of vampires, they are not always portrayed as blood-drinkers, though they do feed on the energy of the living. Some accounts describe them as appearing human, while others depict them as shadowy figures or monstrous beings. Protection against the Moroi involves garlic, holy water, and rituals performed by priests or wise women. Belief in Moroi persists in rural Romania, highlighting the enduring power of folklore and the fear of the unquiet dead. They are considered dangerous and malevolent entities, capable of causing illness, misfortune, and even death.
subtitle: Undead creatures of Romanian folklore.
appearance: Moroi can appear in various forms, from seemingly normal humans to shadowy, monstrous figures. Some accounts describe them as pale and gaunt, while others depict them as having glowing eyes or sharp teeth.
habitat: Moroi are typically found in cemeteries, abandoned buildings, and other places associated with death and decay. They are also believed to haunt the homes of their former victims or those they seek to torment.
behavior: Moroi are generally malevolent creatures, driven by a desire for revenge or to feed on the life force of the living. They are known to torment humans, causing illness, misfortune, and even death. Some accounts suggest they can also possess people.

keyMyths.title: The Moroi and the Unbaptized Child
keyMyths.description: A story tells of a child who died before being baptized and became a Moroi, haunting his family. A priest was called to perform a special ritual to appease the spirit and prevent further harm. The ritual involved burying certain items and reciting specific prayers to release the child's soul. This highlights the importance of religious rites in Romanian folklore to prevent the dead from becoming malevolent.

keyMyths.title: The Moroi and the Village Well
keyMyths.description: A Moroi was said to have poisoned the village well, causing widespread illness. A wise woman discovered the source of the contamination and used herbs and incantations to purify the water, banishing the Moroi from the village. This demonstrates the belief in the Moroi's ability to cause physical harm and the role of folk healers in combating them.

keyMyths.title: The Moroi and the Haunted House
keyMyths.description: A family moved into an old house and was plagued by strange occurrences, attributed to a Moroi. They sought the help of a priest who identified the Moroi as a former resident who had died violently. The priest performed an exorcism to cleanse the house and release the Moroi's spirit, bringing peace to the family. This showcases the Moroi's connection to specific locations and the power of religious intervention.

sources.author: Eliade, Mircea
sources.work: Zalmoxis, the Vanishing God: Comparative Studies in the Religions and Folklore of Dacia and Eastern Europe
sources.description: Provides context for understanding Romanian folklore and religious beliefs, including the role of the undead.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding Romanian folklore and religious beliefs, including the role of the undead.
